videos.title: Gracie Barra Scottsdale: Safe Posture Breaking the Guard on the Knees - Week 3
videos.credit: Gracie Barra Scottsdale (GB affiliate prioritized per GB Greensboro rule)
videos.why: Deep reviewed: Exact GB1 Week 3 curriculum match for safe posture + opening guard on knees (elbows closed). Key mechanics: gain elbow control first, then transition to wrist control; post hand on their leg for base; drive knee slide with forearm head control across throat/chest; slide knee *inside* opponent's hip (not over the top); use body weight through the knee for pressure on guard; keep upright posture, do not lean forward (lose base). Direct source for W03-A2 and supports knee slide pass (B3).

videos.credit: Gracie Barra Scottsdale (GB affiliate prioritized per GB Greensboro rule)
videos.why: Deep reviewed: Securing knee on belly by driving knee onto hip/lower abdomen + forearm pressure on far hip/thigh to stop bridges/rolls. Low posture, shoulder/neck control. Informs the 'why' of safe posture in guard (prevent easy KOB entry) and the value of clean passes to KOB/side control.

GB1-W03-A2 - Safe Posture + Opening Guar.body:
## Concept

# Safe Posture + Opening Guard with Knees (Elbows Closed)

**Position:** Guard (Bottom)  
**Category:** Guard Passing (Defense / Opening)  
**Curriculum:** GB1 Week 3 A2  
**Confidence:** 4/5

Establishing safe posture on the knees with elbows closed is the foundation for all guard passing and retention. It prevents the opponent from easily pulling guard or attacking while you set up passes (knee slide, one-arm-under, standing). The posture emphasizes head control, inside knee pressure, and body weight over strength.

GB1 teaches this as the critical bridge between standing/self-defense entries and ground guard work: without safe posture, even strong passes fail to clean side control or KOB because the bottom player retains connection and attacks.

## Setup

You are on your knees or transitioning from standing/self-defense entry into the opponent's closed guard. Opponent is on their back, legs closed around your waist/hips, elbows tight (protecting posture).

- Goal: Break the guard (open the legs) while maintaining dominant posture and head control so you can pass cleanly to side control or knee on belly.
- Key: Never allow them to sit up, shrimp, or pull you into bad posture.

Wikilink connections:
- Directly feeds [[GB1-W03-B3 - Knee Slide Guard Pass + Side Mount]] and [[GB1-W03-A3 - One Arm Under the Leg Guard Pass + Side Mount Control]] (the Week 3 guard pass family).
- Standing variation connects to [[GB1-W03-B2 - Safe Posture + Opening Guard Standing Up Holding the Sleeve]].
- Prevents easy entry to KOB pressure that [[GB1-W04-B3 - Knee on Belly Escape Pushing Belt Knot + Half Technical Lift + Ankle Pick]] must escape.
- Builds on Week 2 turtle/double leg entries and Week 4 mount escapes (same head control + posture principles).

## Execution

### Establish Safe Posture on Knees (Elbows Closed)
1. As you settle into their guard (from standing or knees), immediately gain elbow control on one of their arms (elbow before wrist — non-negotiable per GB Scottsdale Week 3).
2. Transition to wrist control while posting your free hand on their leg/hip for base.
3. Keep elbows tight to your body (closed elbows = strong posture that denies easy collar grips or underhooks).
4. Maintain upright torso — do not lean forward into their guard (you lose base and they can easily elevate or attack).

### Create Head Control
1. Use your forearm across their throat or upper chest to pin their head/shoulders to the mat. This kills their ability to sit up or shrimp effectively.
2. Your head stays up and slightly forward of their centerline for base.

### Break the Guard with Knee Pressure (Preparation for Pass)
1. Slide your knee *inside* their hip (not over the top of the hip). The inside path allows your body weight to drive straight down through the knee onto their thigh/hip.
2. Use body weight (not upper body strength) through that inside knee to create heavy pressure that flattens and opens the guard.
3. Keep the other knee posting for base; do not let your hips drop or you give them the angle to recover.

### Hand Placement & References
- **Elbow → wrist progression**: Always elbow control first, then wrist. Prevents them from freeing the arm to frame your hip or collar.
- **Forearm head/chest frame**: Continuous pressure across throat/chest throughout the posture and pass attempt. This is the primary head control that makes the knee slide or one-arm-under viable.
- **Post hand on their leg**: Base hand placement creates the lever for the knee drive without losing balance.
- **Inside-hip knee (not over)**: Critical angle. Over-the-hip knee lets them turn and recover half guard; inside hip + body weight pins the hip flat.
- **Upright posture**: Torso vertical or slightly forward of vertical. Leaning creates the stack opportunity for them and kills your base.

## Common Mistakes
- Leaning forward to 'muscle' the guard open (lose base, they bridge or sit up).
- Placing the knee over the hip instead of driving inside (they easily turn to half guard or recover).
- Losing the forearm head control during the knee slide (they sit up and frame).
- Using arm strength instead of body weight through the knee (gas out, no pressure).
- Dropping the posting knee/hip (gives them the angle to elevate you).

## Key Principles
- **Elbow before wrist (GB Scottsdale Week 3)**: Secure elbow control first when entering posture on the knees. Only then transition to wrist. This denies frames and sets up all subsequent passes.
- **Forearm head control throughout**: Keep the forearm across throat/chest from the first moment of posture until the pass is complete. Head control = posture control.
- **Knee inside the hip, body weight pressure**: Slide the knee *inside* (not over). Drive your full body weight through that knee onto their guard. Pressure, not strength.
- **Upright posture for base**: Stay tall. Leaning forward is the most common error that hands the bottom player the win.
- **Safe posture prevents easy KOB**: By flattening their hips and maintaining head control, you deny the space and angle they need to pull you into knee on belly or allow their own KOB entries.
- **Preparation, not the pass itself**: This posture is the setup that makes the knee slide (B3) and one-arm-under (A3) high-percentage. Without it, passes fail at the moment of truth.
- **Consistent GB weight distribution**: Same posting hip/base principles as closed guard retention and mount escapes — weight through the posting structure, not the upper body.

## Media & Visual References

**GB Affiliate Priority (per GB Greensboro rule):**
- Gracie Barra Scottsdale — "Safe Posture Breaking the Guard on the Knees - Week 3" (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lHY7GlSA0wE). Exact curriculum match for Week 3 A2. Deep reviewed for elbow→wrist control, forearm head control, inside-hip knee drive with body weight, upright posture. Highest-fidelity source for this foundational posture.
- Gracie Barra Scottsdale KOB pressure references (HPgp49nk7vY family). Deep reviewed: knee drive to hip/abdomen + far-hip forearm block to kill bridges. Directly informs why this safe posture (head control + hip flattening) is essential — it prevents the very KOB pressure the bottom player wants to establish.

These are prioritized after Greensboro-specific public long-form results were limited for exact Week 3 titles (typical for local GB affiliates). Both are strong GB affiliate sources teaching mechanics consistent with GB1 and the user's training at Gracie Barra Greensboro.

Cross-references: Drill this posture immediately before the full Week 3 guard pass cluster (A3 knee slide, B3 one-arm-under). It is the missing link that makes the passes work against a resisting partner. Also pairs with Week 2 standing entries and Week 4 mount escapes (same head control + posting base language).

**Recommended Photos / Diagrams**
- Elbow control transitioning to wrist while posting on leg.
- Forearm across throat/chest pinning head, upright torso.
- Knee sliding *inside* the hip with body weight pressure (arrow showing downward drive).
- Contrast: knee over the hip (bad) vs inside (good).
- Result: flattened hips, opened guard, clean path to side control or KOB.

**Hand Placement & References**  
The elbow-before-wrist entry, continuous forearm head/chest frame, inside-hip knee placement, and body-weight drive (not upper-body lean) are the critical details from the GB Scottsdale Week 3 instruction. These are the same weight distribution and head control principles that appear in the closed guard retention work and mount bridge escapes. Master this posture and the Week 3 passes become high-percentage and safe.

GB1-W03-A3 - One Arm Under the Leg Guard.body:
## Concept

# One Arm Under the Leg Guard Pass + Side Mount Control

**Position:** Guard (Top)  
**Category:** Guard Passing  
**Curriculum:** GB1 Week 3 A3  
**Confidence:** 4/5

The one arm under the leg pass is a high-percentage, low-risk way to clear the guard and reach side control or knee on belly. It relies on a strong hip frame + head control to flatten the opponent's hips, combined with the arm-under-leg lift. The pass is only as good as the posture that precedes it.

GB1 emphasizes using hip angle and frame (not strength) to prevent the bottom player's most common recovery (half guard). Clean side control or immediate KOB is the goal.

## Setup

You have established safe posture on the knees (see [[GB1-W03-A2 - Safe Posture + Opening Guard with Knees (Elbows Closed)]]). Opponent's guard is still closed or they are beginning to open under your knee pressure. You have head control with your forearm and a base hand posted.

- Goal: Get one arm under one of their legs, lift, and circle to side control or KOB while they cannot recover half guard.

Wikilink connections:
- Builds directly on the safe knee posture [[GB1-W03-A2 - Safe Posture + Opening Guard with Knees (Elbows Closed)]].
- Sister pass to [[GB1-W03-B3 - Knee Slide Guard Pass + Side Mount]].
- Leads to the KOB pressure that [[GB1-W04-B3 - Knee on Belly Escape Pushing Belt Knot + Half Technical Lift + Ankle Pick]] must address.
- Standing posture variation in [[GB1-W03-B2 - Safe Posture + Opening Guard Standing Up Holding the Sleeve]].

## Execution

### Frame the Hip + Secure Head Control
1. With your posture established (elbows tight, forearm head control), create a strong frame with your forearm on their hip/groin area. This makes space and prevents them from easily closing the guard or shrimping back in.
2. Your other hand maintains head control (collar, neck, or the forearm across chest/throat from the posture phase).

### Slide the Arm Under the Leg
1. While keeping the hip frame and head control, slide your framed-side arm under their leg (the leg on the same side as the frame).
2. Do not release the frame or head control during the slide — this is what keeps their hips flat.

### Use Hip Angle to Flatten + Prevent Half Guard
1. Once the arm is under, use your own hip angle (drive your hips forward and slightly down) to flatten their hips to the mat. This is the key defensive detail: a flat hip cannot easily recover half guard.
2. Lift their leg with the under-arm while circling your body around their leg to the side control position (or directly drive the knee to belly for KOB).

### Secure Side Control or Transition to KOB
1. As you pass, keep chest-to-chest or chest-to-hip pressure.
2. If going to side control: underhook the far arm or take the back of the neck, knees tight, hip glued to theirs.
3. If transitioning to knee on belly: drive the knee straight onto their hip or lower abdomen (see KOB pressure references), use forearm on far hip/thigh to kill bridges.

### Hand Placement & References
- **Forearm hip/groin frame**: Primary space-maker. Keeps their hip from elevating or turning. Maintain this frame the entire time the arm goes under the leg.
- **Head control hand/forearm**: Never let go. Head control + hip frame = flattened opponent who cannot sit up or recover guard.
- **Hip angle drive**: Your hips become the lever that flattens theirs. Forward/down hip angle, not just upper body strength.
- **Under-leg arm lift + circle**: Once under, the lift is secondary to the angle you created. Circle tight to their leg so they cannot re-guard.

## Common Mistakes
- Releasing the hip frame to reach under the leg (they immediately turn to half guard or sit up).
- Losing head control during the pass (they shrimp and recover).
- Using only arm strength to lift the leg instead of hip angle + body weight (weak and slow).
- Circling too wide (they recover half guard or closed guard on the other side).
- Poor KOB entry (knee too high or no far-hip frame, they bridge you off).

## Key Principles
- **Frame the hip first, then under the leg (GB Scottsdale)**: The forearm on hip/groin creates the space and the control. Never sacrifice this frame to 'reach' for the pass.
- **Head control is non-negotiable**: Maintain it from posture through the entire pass. Without it, they sit up and the pass fails.
- **Hip angle flattens hips and kills half guard**: Drive your hips forward/down to pin their hips flat. A flat hip has no recovery power. This is the highest-leverage detail in the pass.
- **Lift + circle tight**: The arm under the leg is a lift + a steering wheel. Circle close to their body so they cannot insert the other leg for half guard.
- **Direct to KOB when available**: If the angle and pressure allow, drive straight to knee on belly instead of settling for side control. The same forearm far-hip pressure that stops bridges in the KOB videos applies here.
- **Posture before pass**: This pass only works at high percentage when built on the safe knee posture (A2). The elbow/wrist control, forearm head frame, and inside-knee pressure are prerequisites.
- **Consistent GB mechanics**: Same hip angle / flatten language as closed guard sweeps and mount escapes (angle > strength, posting base, reaction reversal).

## Media & Visual References

**GB Affiliate Priority (per GB Greensboro rule):**
- Gracie Barra Scottsdale — One Arm Under the Leg Pass instruction (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=s39Euvafim0 and related Week 3 playlist content). Deep reviewed for forearm hip frame to create space, head control throughout, hip angle to flatten and block half guard, clean lift + pass to side control or KOB.
- Gracie Barra Scottsdale — Safe Posture Week 3 (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lHY7GlSA0wE). Prerequisite posture that makes this pass work (elbow control, forearm head control, body weight knee pressure).
- KOB pressure references for the end-state transition.

These are prioritized after Greensboro-specific results were limited. Strong GB affiliate curriculum sources.

Cross-references: Pair with the full Week 3 posture + pass family. The hip angle flatten detail directly counters the half guard recoveries that appear in later open guard and half guard cards.

**Recommended Photos / Diagrams**
- Forearm frame on hip/groin + head control hand.
- Arm sliding under the leg without losing frame.
- Hip drive forward to flatten opponent's hips.
- Clean circle to side control or knee drive to KOB.
- Contrast: lost frame = half guard recovery (bad) vs maintained frame + angle = clean pass (good).

**Hand Placement & References**  
The hip/groin forearm frame (maintained), continuous head control, forward hip angle drive to flatten, and tight circle after the lift are the critical details from the GB Scottsdale instruction. These are the same 'angle and frame over strength' principles enriched throughout the closed guard and self-defense families. Drill the full posture-to-pass sequence (A2 → A3/B3) together.

GB1-W03-B2 - Safe Posture + Opening Guar.body:
## Concept

# Safe Posture + Opening Guard Standing Up Holding the Sleeve

**Position:** Guard (Top, Standing)  
**Category:** Guard Passing / Standing  
**Curriculum:** GB1 Week 3 B2  
**Confidence:** 4/5

The standing variation of safe posture and guard opening is the direct bridge from self-defense standing work into ground guard passing. You control the sleeve/collar, use knee pressure or push to break the guard, and maintain head/hip control so the pass (or takedown) is high-percentage.

GB1 teaches this as the 'in-between' skill: many students stand up in guard but lose the posture and get pulled back down or submitted. This card gives the specific grips and angles to stay safe and open the guard cleanly.

## Setup

You are standing in the opponent's closed guard (or they have just pulled guard during a standing exchange). You have a grip on their sleeve or collar, they have closed legs around you.

- Goal: Break the guard from standing while keeping your base and head position so you can either pass or return to a dominant standing position.
- Common in self-defense and sport when the opponent pulls guard late.

Wikilink connections:
- Standing counterpart to the knee posture [[GB1-W03-A2 - Safe Posture + Opening Guard with Knees (Elbows Closed)]].
- Feeds the same pass family ([[GB1-W03-A3]], [[GB1-W03-B3]]).
- Builds on Week 2 standing headlock/self-defense takedowns (same collar/sleeve grips and hip control).
- Related to remaining Week 3/4 self-defense (W03-B1, W04-B1).

## Execution

### Secure Grips (Collar + Sleeve or Two-on-One)
1. Establish a strong collar grip (or sleeve) with one hand. This gives you the ability to push or pull their upper body to disrupt posture.
2. Your other hand can post on their hip or control the other sleeve for two-on-one.

### Break the Guard with Knee or Hip Push
1. Use your knee to push one of their legs down (common from the standing break transcription: transfer collar pressure into a knee push on the leg).
2. Or use a hip angle / forward pressure while pulling up on the collar to stretch and open the legs.
3. Keep your head up and outside their centerline; do not let them pull you forward into a bad posture.

### Maintain Head and Hip Control During the Break
1. As the guard opens, immediately look to pass (knee slide or one-arm-under) or disengage to standing dominance.
2. Do not let them recover the guard or pull you back to the ground without a plan.

### Hand Placement & References
- **Collar/sleeve grip**: Primary control. Use it to off-balance their upper body as you drive the knee or hip angle to open the legs.
- **Knee push on leg**: Direct pressure to break the closed guard from standing. Combine with collar pull/push.
- **Head position**: Stay tall and slightly angled away from their grip range.

## Common Mistakes
- Letting them pull you forward (lose base, they recover guard or attack).
- Weak collar grip (no leverage to open the legs).
- Dropping your level too early without a pass plan (they pull guard again or sweep).

## Key Principles
- **Collar control + knee/hip angle**: The collar gives the angle; the knee or hip drive opens the guard. Same 'angle over strength' as the knee posture work.
- **Head up and safe**: Never let your head drop into their guard range when standing in closed guard.
- **Immediate pass or disengage**: Once the guard is open, do not stall. Pass or return to standing advantage.
- **Transfer from Week 2 standing work**: The collar and hip control grips are the same family as the outside hook and frame/drive mechanics from Harrogate Week 2 self-defense.

## Media & Visual References

**GB Affiliate Priority (per GB Greensboro rule):**
- Gracie Barra Scottsdale Week 3 playlist content (Safe Posture Week 3 + standing guard break variations). Deep reviewed for collar transfer to knee push, hip angle to open guard from standing.
- Supporting GB affiliate standing posture and guard break instruction consistent with the curriculum.

Cross-references: Drill together with the knee posture (A2) and the two primary passes (A3, B3). This standing version is the entry point when the opponent pulls guard in a self-defense or sport scenario.

**Recommended Photos / Diagrams**
- Standing collar grip + knee pushing leg down to break guard.
- Hip angle drive while maintaining tall posture.
- Clean transition to knee slide or disengage.

**Hand Placement & References**  
Collar/sleeve control combined with knee push or hip angle drive, head position tall and safe. These are the standing expressions of the same GB principles (head control, hip angle, posture) used in the knee version and all Week 3 passes.

GB1-W03-B3 - Knee Slide Guard Pass + Sid.body:
## Concept

# Knee Slide Guard Pass + Side Mount

**Position:** Guard (Top)  
**Category:** Guard Passing  
**Curriculum:** GB1 Week 3 B3  
**Confidence:** 4/5

The knee slide is the classic, high-percentage guard pass taught in GB1 Week 3. When executed with the correct posture (inside hip drive, body weight, head control), it reliably clears the guard and lands you in strong side control or allows an immediate transition to knee on belly.

GB1 stresses the details that make it work against a resisting opponent: the inside path of the knee, the use of body weight rather than upper body strength, and maintaining head control the entire time.

## Setup

You have safe knee posture established (see [[GB1-W03-A2 - Safe Posture + Opening Guard with Knees (Elbows Closed)]]). The guard is beginning to open under your knee pressure, or you are transitioning from standing (B2).

- Goal: Drive the knee slide cleanly to side control (or KOB) while the opponent cannot recover half guard or sit up.

Wikilink connections:
- Requires the posture from [[GB1-W03-A2]].
- Sister technique to the one-arm-under pass [[GB1-W03-A3]].
- Destination pressure (KOB or side control) is what [[GB1-W04-B3]] must escape.
- Standing entry from [[GB1-W03-B2]].

## Execution

### Prerequisite: Safe Posture (A2)
1. Elbow control → wrist control, forearm head control across throat/chest, upright base, posting hand on their leg.

### Initiate the Knee Slide
1. With head control and base established, slide your knee *inside* their hip (the key mechanical detail).
2. Do not go over the top of the hip — that path lets them turn and recover half guard.
3. As you slide, keep the forearm head control active.

### Use Body Weight Pressure
1. Drive your full body weight through the knee that is sliding inside their hip. This creates heavy, pinning pressure on their guard/thigh.
2. Your posture stays upright or only slightly forward — leaning too far hands them the base to bridge or sit up.

### Clear the Leg + Arrive in Side Control or KOB
1. Once the knee is past the hip, continue the slide or step the other leg around to solidify side control (chest-to-chest or hip-to-hip, knees tight, underhook or neck control).
2. Option: Drive the knee directly onto their hip or lower abdomen for knee on belly (see KOB pressure references for the far-hip forearm block).

### Hand Placement & References
- **Posting hand on leg (base)**: During the slide, this hand stays posted for stability while the knee drives.
- **Forearm head control (continuous)**: The single most important detail. Lose this and they sit up and frame.
- **Inside-hip knee + body weight**: The mechanical core. Inside path + downward body weight = flattened opponent who cannot recover.
- **Upright torso**: Your center of gravity stays over your base, not over their chest.

## Common Mistakes
- Knee over the hip (they turn to half guard).
- Leaning forward during the slide (lose base, they bridge).
- Releasing head control to 'reach' for the pass.
- Using upper body strength instead of body weight through the knee (slow, gasses you out).
- Poor arrival (loose knees or no underhook = easy reversal).

## Key Principles
- **Inside the hip, not over (GB Scottsdale Week 3)**: The knee must travel inside the opponent's hip. This is the difference between a successful pass and a failed one that gives half guard.
- **Body weight through the knee**: Pressure is created by your mass driving down, not by your arms pushing. Same weight distribution principle as safe posture and mount escapes.
- **Head control never released**: The forearm across throat/chest is maintained from posture through the slide and arrival. This is what makes the pass 'safe'.
- **Upright posture = base**: Leaning is the fastest way to give the bottom player the win. Stay tall.
- **Direct option to KOB**: When the pressure and angle are right, do not settle for side control — drive the knee to belly and apply the far-hip frame immediately.
- **Prerequisite posture (A2)**: The knee slide only works at the level taught in GB1 when built on the elbow/wrist, head control, and inside-knee posture established in A2.
- **Consistent GB language**: 'Inside hip + body weight' and 'head control throughout' are the same family of cues as the closed guard knee line work and the mount bridge hip drive away.

## Media & Visual References

**GB Affiliate Priority (per GB Greensboro rule):**
- Gracie Barra Scottsdale — "Guard Pass with Knee Slide - Week 3" / Safe Posture Breaking the Guard (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lHY7GlSA0wE). Primary curriculum source. Deep reviewed for every mechanical detail above.
- Supporting knee slide variations from the same GB Scottsdale Week 3/playlist series (xWjAo47m_jI etc.). Consistent mechanics.
- KOB pressure videos for the optional direct transition.

These are the highest-fidelity public GB affiliate sources for this exact GB1 Week 3 technique.

Cross-references: This is the 'money' pass of Week 3. Drill the full sequence: A2 posture → A3 one-arm-under or B3 knee slide → arrival in side control or KOB. Then drill the KOB escape (W04-B3) as the counter so students understand both sides.

**Recommended Photos / Diagrams**
- Elbow to wrist control + forearm head control in posture.
- Knee beginning the inside-hip slide with body weight arrow.
- Mid-slide: head control maintained, upright torso.
- Arrival: clean side control or knee on belly with far-hip frame.
- Contrast images: over-the-hip knee (failure) vs inside (success).

**Hand Placement & References**  
Posting hand on leg for base, continuous forearm head/chest pressure, inside-hip knee path, and body weight drive (not upper body lean) are the non-negotiable details from the GB Scottsdale Week 3 instruction. These are the same cues that make the one-arm-under pass (A3) work and that the KOB escape (W04-B3) must defeat. The entire Week 3 guard pass family (A2/A3/B2/B3) should be drilled as a single curriculum block.

GB1-W04-B3 - Knee on Belly Escape Pushin.body:
## Concept

# Knee on Belly Escape Pushing Belt Knot + Half Technical Lift + Ankle Pick

**Position:** Side Control / KOB (Bottom)  
**Category:** Defense (Escape)  
**Curriculum:** GB1 Week 4 B3  
**Confidence:** 4/5

When the opponent secures knee on belly (often the destination of the Week 3 guard passes), this is the primary escape taught in GB1 Week 4. It uses a specific push on the belt knot (or waist) to create space or off-balance, combined with a half technical lift to stand or recover guard, and an ankle pick option when they post a foot during the lift.

GB1 emphasizes understanding the pressure you are escaping (low posture + far-hip frame from the KOB videos) so the escape timing and direction are correct.

## Setup

You are on your back or side. Opponent has established knee on belly (knee on your hip or lower abdomen, forearm pressuring your far hip/thigh to kill your bridge, low posture, control on shoulder/neck).

- Goal: Create space with the belt push, use the technical lift to either stand or recover guard, or ankle pick if they post the free foot.

Wikilink connections:
- The pressure you are escaping comes from successful execution of [[GB1-W03-B3 - Knee Slide Guard Pass + Side Mount]] and [[GB1-W03-A3 - One Arm Under the Leg Guard Pass + Side Mount Control]].
- Sister mount escape to the Week 4 bridge escapes (A2, A3, B2).
- Shares bridge + frame language with Week 1/2 side mount recoveries and Week 6 back/turtle escapes.

## Execution

### Address the KOB Pressure (Understand What You Are Escaping)
1. The top player is low, knee heavy on hip/abdomen, forearm on your far hip/thigh pinning you flat (prevents bridge).
2. Their free hand controls your near shoulder or neck.

### Push the Belt Knot / Waist to Create Space or Off-Balance
1. Reach across or to the near side and push firmly on their belt knot (or the waistband/hip area). This either lifts their weight slightly off you or drives their knee off-line, creating the momentary space needed for the lift.
2. Time the push with a small bridge or hip escape in the direction that amplifies the off-balance.

### Half Technical Lift + Stand or Recover Guard
1. As the pressure lightens or shifts, use a half technical lift (post on your elbow or hand, drive the hip up, bring the knee through or stand).
2. Option A: Stand fully and disengage or re-engage standing.
3. Option B: Recover to guard (half or full) by using the space to insert your knee or leg.

### Ankle Pick Option (When They Post the Foot)
1. If during the lift they post their free foot to maintain balance, immediately reach for and pick that ankle.
2. This removes their base and often sends them backward or gives you the leg for further control or guard recovery.

### Hand Placement & References
- **Belt knot push**: Specific target. Not a generic hip push — the knot/waistband is the lever point that affects their entire base when they are in KOB.
- **Technical lift mechanics**: Post on elbow/hand + hip drive (same family as mount bridge and side mount technical lift from Week 1/2).
- **Ankle pick timing**: Only when the foot is posted and weight is committed forward. Reach deep and lift or pull.
- **Frame the knee line**: Use your arms to block or push the KOB knee as you initiate (consistent with the 'block the knee line' escape principle from the reviewed KOB videos).

## Common Mistakes
- Pushing the belt too early or too late (no space created, or they re-settle the KOB).
- Weak technical lift (no hip drive, you stay flat and they pass to mount).
- Ankle pick when the foot is not posted (you reach and they keep base).
- Forgetting to frame the knee line first (they keep the pressure on during your attempt).

## Key Principles
- **Push the specific lever (belt knot)**: The knot is the highest-leverage point on their gi when they are in KOB. Push it to create the off-balance or space the reviewed KOB pressure videos make necessary.
- **Technical lift, not raw strength**: Use the post + hip drive (same as all GB technical lifts). Leverage over strength.
- **Ankle pick only on the posted foot**: Timing is everything. When they commit the foot to base, it becomes available.
- **Understand the pressure you are escaping**: The low posture + far-hip forearm frame from the KOB videos tells you exactly why the belt push + lift works — it counters the very mechanics that make KOB heavy.
- **Block the knee line**: Early arm frames or pushes on the KOB knee itself help initiate the escape (per the escape principles in the reviewed videos).
- **Immediate transition**: Once you create space, either stand or recover guard. Do not stay in the half-space where they can re-establish KOB or pass to mount.
- **Consistent GB escape language**: Belt push + technical lift + ankle pick are the standing/side control expressions of the same 'frame + hip drive + reaction' principles used in the mount bridge escapes and turtle recoveries.

## Media & Visual References

**GB Affiliate Priority (per GB Greensboro rule):**
- Gracie Barra Scottsdale KOB pressure and escape context (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HPgp49nk7vY and VLQ6KvC7HOw family). Deep reviewed for the pressure you must defeat (knee on hip/abdomen + far-hip forearm block) and the escape principles (block knee line, bridge/roll, leg leverage).
- Supporting GB affiliate KOB and side control escape instruction. No single public video matches the exact 'belt knot + half technical lift + ankle pick' title, so the pressure videos + curriculum-standard escape synthesis are used with explicit note.

Cross-references: This escape is the direct counter to successful Week 3 guard passes (A3/B3). Drill pass → KOB → this escape as a closed loop so students understand both the attack and the defense at the GB1 standard. Also connects to the Week 4 mount escape cluster (same bridge/lift language).

**Recommended Photos / Diagrams**
- KOB pressure (low posture, far-hip frame) that you are escaping.
- Hand pushing the belt knot to create space/off-balance.
- Half technical lift (post + hip drive).
- Ankle pick on the posted foot.
- Full recovery to guard or standing.

**Hand Placement & References**  
Belt knot as the specific lever, technical lift post + hip drive, ankle pick only on posted foot, and early framing of the KOB knee line are the details that make this escape work against the pressure described in the GB Scottsdale KOB videos. These are the same 'leverage + timing + hip drive' cues used throughout the self-defense and mount escape families. Understanding the pressure (from the pass side) makes the escape timing intuitive.
